text:
National Aeronautics and Space Administration 
NASA SAFETY CENTER 
SYSTEM FAILURE CASE STUDY 
PROXIMATE CAUSE 
• Spurious signals may have been 
generated when the lander legs 
were deployed during descent, 
resulting in premature shutdown of 
the lander engines
UNDERLYING ISSUES 
• Design, Review, and Verification  
and Validation
• Project Implementation and  
Demand Margins
AFTERMATH 
• Creation of a dedicated JPL Mars  
Program Office that reported  
directly to the Laboratory Director  
• Creation of an independent  
organization at the Directorate  
level dedicated to implementing  
major flight projects
• Recognition of the FBC concept  
as relevant and recommendation  
as the continued approach  
utilized in the Mars Program, with  
the stipulation that FBC should  
not compromise safety
September  2012  Volume  6  Issue  8 
Lost Lander 
Failure of the Mars Polar Lander 
January 3, 1999: The Mars Polar (MP L) wa s launched from Cape Canaveral, FL,  and began 
an 11-month cruise to Mars, where  it was to study soil and climate hi story in the southern  
polar Planum Australe region during its 90- day mission. MPL approached M ars on 
December 3, 1999, a ccording to schedule and aft er final trajectory corrections, achi eved 
correct entry attitude for descent into the  Martian atmosphere. An acce pted risk by 
the project, the MPL antennae would not b e aligned with Earth, an d an expected loss 
of communication would occur during the entry, descent, an d landing (EDL) sequence. 
Controller s expected to reestablish communication at 20: 39:00 Co ordinated Universal 
Time (UTC), after MPL had landed. However, all attempts at reestablishing communication 
with the lander failed. 
Background
Design 
Encased in an aerodynamic entry  
vehicle comprising a forward heatshield  
and a backshell, the lander separated  
from the cruise stage of its launch  
vehicle approximately 5 minutes before  
atmospheric entry. During the EDL  
sequence, MPL was designed to deploy  
a parachute and jettison its heatshield.  
During descent, MPL would then release  
two Deep Space 2 (DS2) microprobes as  
part of an add-on mission designed to  
collect soil, meteorology, and seismic data.  
After release of the DS2 microprobes, MPL  
would deploy its landing legs and confirm  
ground acquisition by radar.  
After acquisition, the lander was  
designed to separate from its backshell  
and parachute and begin powered  
descent to the surface. The complete  
EDL sequence took approximately 5.5  
minutes to complete. After landing, the  
solar-powered MPL was to send data to  
the Mars Climate Orbiter (MCO) for relay  
to Earth; however, the failure of MCO on  
September 23, 1999, left MPL to use its  
x-band radio and medium-gain antenna  
(MGA) in conjunction with the Deep  
Space Network (DSN) antenna array to  
communicate with operators on Earth.
What  happened
W
ithout communication and EDL geometry 
data, it was impossible to know whether 
www.nasa.gov 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
Figure 1. Arrangement of MPL landing components. Source: NASA 
MPL reached its powered descent propulsion phase. If it  
did reach this phase, one possible failure scenario illustrated  
premature engine shutdown resulting in a crash landing. 
Also of note, the DS2 microprobes—designed to impact with  
the Martian surface without using a device to slow their rate  
of descent—were lost after communication attempts with the  
microprobes failed. 
proximate  cause
Independent investigators determined that a key issue that may 
have caused the failure was spurious signals generated when 
the lander legs were deployed during descent. The spurious 
signals gave a false indication of a landing, resulting in premature 
shutdown of the lander engines and the destruction of the lander 
when it crashed into the Martian surface. This finding was not 
confirmed because of the lack of data transmitted during EDL. 
underlying  issues
While software and hardware design played a major role in 
the failure of MPL, they correlate with project implementation 
concerns and program-level communication issues. 
Design, Review, and Verification and 
Validation 
At the time MPL was designed and built, there was a known 
problem for sensors involved with mechanical operations, 
such as lander leg deployment, to produce spurious signals. 
For MPL, no software requirement existed to clear spurious 
signals prior to the lander using the sensor information to 
confirm successful landing. During tests of the lander system, 
the sensors were incorrectly wired because of a design error. 
As a result, the systems test did not identify spurious signals 
and was not repeated with properly wired touchdown sensors. 
Furthermore, the Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL), which 
managed the MPL project, noted in its investigation report 
that certain failure modes (e.g., transient signals) were not 
specifically outlined in the system-level requirement document. 
While the most probable cause of the failure was premature 
engine shutdown, an underlying issue was inadequate software 
design and testing. 
Project Implementation and Demand 
Margins 
From the onset, the MPL project, a component of the  
overarching Mars ’98 project, was under significant cost risk,  
partly because of an aggressive proposal from contractor  
Lockheed Martin Astronautics (LMA). Adding to this situation,  
JPL believed that no cost increase was possible, even after MPL  
scientific payload requirements began to creep. Investigators  
later estimated at least a 30-percent underfunding. 
In contrast, the highly successful and well-managed Pathfinder  
mission possessed a project management budget of $11  
million while the entire Mars ’98 program (including MCO  
[which also failed] and MPL) allocated only $5 million. The  
Pathfinder budget for Science and Instrument Development  
(SID) was $14 million, compared with the overall $37 million  
SID budget for Mars ’98. 
In 1999, JPL was tasked with a considerable number of  
projects; the Mars Program Independent Assessment  
Team (MPIAT) described a tremendous workload on JPL  
management. As a result of a 3-year overhaul to the Faster,  
Better, Cheaper (FBC) initiative in the late 1990s, JPL had gone  
from averaging 1 to 4 projects in a given year to a level of 10  
Figure 2. Although the Pathfinder mission involed an active rover, 
the Mars ‘98 project suite’s SID budget was more than twice that of 
Pathfinder, utilizing less than half of Pathfinders Project Mangement 
budget. Source: NASA. 
2 | Page System Failure Case Studies - Lost Lander September 2012 
 
 
 
 
 
to 15 concurrent projects. This profusion of projects translated  
into an understaffed project office. The MPL project brought  
in highly competent, yet inexperienced project managers, a  
personnel scenario that called for significant involvement by  
senior management to compensate for the lack of experience.  
However, given so many projects, senior management could  
not focus its attention completely on MPL, and the result  
was an inadequate application of JPL institutional capability  
through the period of transition to FBC. Insufficient checks and  
balances in MPL development and testing resulted from this  
inattention. 
The MPL staff faced tight margins including a limited selection  
of launch vehicles, growth in the MPL scientific payload  
requirements, and a fixed planetary launch window. The sum  
of these resulted in analysis and testing deficiencies and  
inadequate preparation for mission operations.  
The MPIAT found that “JPL management was attempting to  
convey general program advocacy and to promote a positive  
customer relationship, motivated by fear of losing business.  
The result was that JPL management did not convey an  
adequate risk assessment to NASA Headquarters. NASA  
Headquarters interpretation was that JPL was agreeing with  
and accepting objectives, requirements, and constraints.  
This communication dynamic prevented open and effective  
discussion of problems and issues. JPL management did  
not effectively express their concerns to NASA Headquarters  
about programmatic constraints, and NASA Headquarters  
did not seem receptive to receiving bad news. Consequently,  
frank discussions identifying and managing program risks and  
problems did not occur.” 
The communication dynamic bypassed the discussion of  
further testing due to project funding concerns; testing may  
Figure 3. The Boeing Integrated Defense Systems Delta II 7425 launch 
vehicle was chosen to send to MPL to Mars; MPL is pictured being 
placed in the payload fairing of the vehicle. Source: NASA 
Figure 4. KSC technicians check underneath MPL as it sits on a 
workstand in a laboratory cleanroom. Source: NASA 
have identified the software issue regarding identifying and 
clearing spurious signals. 
aftermath
Formation of the MPIAT 
The MPIAT was established as a result of the September 23,  
1999 loss of MCO and the December 3, 1999 loss of MPL and  
DS2 microprobes. After conducting the MPL and DS2 failure  
reviews, the MPIAT identified Lessons Learned from various  
successes and failures within the Mars Program. With these  
lessons, the MPIAT was tasked with reviewing the revised  
Mars Surveyor Program to assure Lessons Learned from  
the previous two failures had been observed and applied at  
JPL. The MPIAT found the failure to be the result of ineffective  
implementation of FBC, as opposed to FBC itself, and that  
cutting testing and validation of system hardware and software  
was not a legitimate method to decrease cost or reduce  
timetables. The FBC approach allows for taking acceptable  
risk in relation to using unproven, high gain technology and not  
risk associated with the reduction of testing. 
In addition, MPIAT recommended that JPL create a Mars  
Program Office reporting directly to the Laboratory Director  
and an independent organization at the Directorate level  
dedicated to implementing major flight projects. 
In 2002, an MPL Lesson Learned later described the software  
failure in three phases. First, in the requirements phase,  
requirements from NASA Headquarters were not clearly  
allocated at a system level; the interface with the touchdown  
sensor did not address transients. Second, the software code  
and test phase missed a programmer-entered logic error.  
Further, the simulation did not model touchdown transient  
signals. Finally, in the system verification phase, an unrelated  
touchdown sensor wiring issue masked the software issue.  
The complete end-to-end test was not repeated and the  
software flaw remained. 
September 2012 System Failur
e Case Studies - Lost Lander 3 | Page 
relevance  to  nasa 
Software Assurance 
Of particular note is the JPL response to software verification and  
validation. The official JPL investigation report identified possible  
physical hazards for all phases of the EDL sequence; however,  
the one statement that concerned software—common to all EDL  
phases—read “Flight software fails to execute properly.” This  
broad statement, if applied to hardware, would have solicited  
more hazard detail at a time when the criticality of software was  
still being learned. 
Other examples attest to a lack of importance placed on critical  
software assurance (e.g., issues with Space Shuttle Endeavor  
and the STS-126 crew with a software failure preventing  
automatic communications hardware reconfiguration; repeated  
and unexpected Mars Pathfinder system resets; Patriot Missile  
targeting and tracking failures during deployment in the early  
1990s [read about these software issues and more in January  
2012 Volume 6 Issue 2 System Failure Case Study Critical  
Software: Good Design Built Right  available at nsc.nasa.gov/ 
Articles/SFCS]). 
More accurately, software does not fail, it “does not perform as  
intended.” A thorough understanding of software behavior needs  
to be mapped, including all possibilities for unintended inputs and  
outputs, because software has a system interface with every  
aspect of NASA flight hardware.  
Verification and Validation 
FBC encouraged prudent risk-taking when justified by the return  
and after extensive review. MPIAT found it to be an effective  
concept for guiding program implementation. However, risk  
associated with deviating from sound principals such as  
thorough verification and validation testing is unacceptable.  
The MPIAT believed that the FBC concept should be the  
continued approach utilized in the Mars Program with the  
stipulation that FBC should not compromise safety. 
The Mars ’98 project did achieve certain successes with its  
limited resources, but deviated significantly from a paragon  
example of analysis and testing. The FBC stratagem involved  
cheaper, smaller, and more technologically risky spacecraft in  
higher quantities to hedge losses in case some craft failed.  
In comparison, NASA’s latest victory with Curiosity, the Mars  
Science Laboratory (MSL) lander, was held back 2 years to  
complete rigorous software and hardware testing required for  
the highly advanced mission. 
RefeRences 
Euler, Edward A., and Steven D. Jolly. 2002, “Recovering from the  
Mars ‘98 Failures: Lessons Learned and Applied.” 2002 MAPLD  
Conference.

Lander and Deep Space 2 Missions . March 22, 2000. 
Leveson, Nancy G. 2000. “The Role of Software in Recent  
Aerospace Accidents.” Proceedings of the 18th International  
System Safety Conference.
